conflicts.description: Chapter 15 frames Storm Mouth as answerable to one heir and one body, but chapters 16-17 reveal a five-point lock that only stabilizes under shared sequence and labor.
conflicts.canonical_value: Storm Mouth is a five-point lock; no single heir can hold it alone.
conflicts.resolution_note: The later chapters embed the mechanism physically and the sea road only opens once all five sibling lines answer it, so Daro's one-heir doctrine is rejected.

conflicts.description: Daro argues that song must come first to name the path, but the successful final sequence requires key, marks, rope, and weight before song.
conflicts.canonical_value: Song comes last in the Storm Mouth sequence, after the other stations are engaged.
conflicts.resolution_note: The chapel machinery begins to hold only when the shared mechanical steps are set first; Daro's early-song approach worsens the pull.

conflicts.description: Nami's private seed is treated as possible proof that the river singled her out, but Salt Room and the final rite establish that it would have rooted in any warm pocket and is not evidence of chosenness.
conflicts.canonical_value: Nami's seed reflects pressure and desire, not a unique divine claim.
conflicts.resolution_note: Bato's explanation and the chapter_17 confession make the world rule explicit, so chosenness stays a character temptation rather than a canon fact.

conflicts.description: The old house begins the cycle as a rotting shrine-home, but chapter 18 rejects reviving it as a saint shrine and rebuilds it as a working communal crossing.
conflicts.canonical_value: House of Oaths/Knot Bend is a rebuilt freight and burial crossing with public tools and maintenance books, not a revived shrine.
conflicts.resolution_note: The final chapter repeatedly argues this end-state on-page and physically implements it, so future continuity should use the rebuilt crossing as the canonical status.
